source.definition: to grasp, keep, or stop something from moving or escaping; to maintain control or possession

explanation.sourceText: The English verb "hold" originates from Old English "healdan", meaning to grasp or keep, which is related to similar words in other Germanic languages.
explanation.targetText: Cuvântul englezesc „hold“ provine din engleza veche „healdan“, care înseamnă a prinde sau a ține, fiind legat de cuvinte similare din alte limbi germanice.

source.additional: The verb is used in many contexts, both physical (to grasp something) and figurative (to maintain an opinion).

target.definition: a menține sau bloca ceva; a nu lăsa să scape

explanation.sourceText: The Romanian verb „a ține“ derives from the Latin „tenere“, meaning to hold or keep.
explanation.targetText: Verbul românesc „a ține“ provine din latină „tenere“, care înseamnă „a ține“ sau „a păstra“.

target.additional: Es wird häufig in der Definition von Eigentum, Verpflichtungen oder Meinungen verwendet.

explanation.sourceText: The act of enclosing or firmly holding something.
explanation.targetText: A prinde sau a ține ceva strâns.

examples.sourceText: I can't let go of the thought.
examples.targetText: Nu pot să scap de gând.

explanation.sourceText: In relation to organizing a session or appointment.
explanation.targetText: Referitor la organizarea unei întâlniri sau eveniment.

examples.sourceText: We are holding the meeting on Wednesday.
I hold the book in my hand.
I will hold the next class.

learningTips.text: Think of 'hold' as keeping something firmly in your hands, just like 'a ține' means to keep or hold in Romanian.
Remember the phrase 'hold on' to help recall 'a ține' when it means to maintain or keep something.
The English word 'hold' sounds like 'hole-d'—imagine holding something so tight it leaves a hole in your hand, linking to 'a ține'.
Associate 'hold' with 'tenere' (Latin root) to connect the English and Romanian verbs etymologically.
